title: Animals Need You Every Day
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Read Aloud / Reference] Storey's Guide to Raising Chickens
required.author: Gail Damerow

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Storey's Guide to Raising Rabbits
supplementary.author: Bob Bennett

competence_markers.description: The child reliably performs daily animal care (water, feed, egg collection) for a minimum of one month with adult supervision
The child can pick up and handle at least one species of animal correctly and calmly
The child can describe what a healthy animal looks like and name at least three signs that something might be wrong
The child understands that eggs come from chickens and meat comes from animals, and can discuss this honestly
The child understands the five basic needs of animals in their care (water, food, shelter, space, predator protection)
The child maintains a daily care log or can narrate their daily routine accurately

content.why_this_matters:
Animals are not toys. They are not decorations. They are living beings that depend on you completely for their food, water, shelter, and safety. When you take responsibility for an animal — even a single chicken — you are making a promise that you will care for it every single day, whether you feel like it or not.

This is different from anything else you have done so far. A garden bed can survive a missed day of watering. A compost pile does not starve if you forget to turn it. But an animal that has no water on a hot day is suffering. An animal that has no food is hungry. An animal left unprotected from a predator can be killed overnight. The responsibility is real, it is daily, and it does not take days off.

This is also one of the most rewarding relationships you will ever have. The chicken that comes running when it sees you carrying the feed bucket knows you. The rabbit that relaxes in your arms when you hold it correctly trusts you. The animals you care for will teach you things about patience, observation, and responsibility that nothing else can teach — because the feedback is immediate, honest, and alive.

Every farmer, every rancher, every person who has ever fed their family from the land understood this: the animals come first. Before your breakfast, before your plans for the day, the animals eat and drink. That is the deal. And keeping that deal, every single day, is one of the most powerful things a young person can learn.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Daily animal care responsibility** — the child feeds, waters, and checks on animals as a non-negotiable daily routine
2. **Animal observation skills** — recognizing normal behavior, signs of health, and signs of distress
3. **Correct animal handling** — picking up, carrying, and interacting with animals safely and gently
4. **Understanding where food comes from** — the honest connection between living animals and the food they produce
5. **Basic welfare standards** — clean water, appropriate food, shelter, space, and protection from predators
6. **The concept of unconditional responsibility** — animals depend on you regardless of your mood, schedule, or preferences
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Meeting the Animals

Before you start caring for animals, you need to understand what they are and what they need.

**Chickens** are birds. They are descended from jungle fowl — wild birds that still live in the forests of Southeast Asia. A chicken is not a pet in the way a dog or cat is a pet, but chickens are smart, social, and curious. They have a social order (called the pecking order — that phrase comes from real chicken behavior). They dust-bathe to keep their feathers clean. They scratch the ground to find insects and seeds. They have personalities: some are bold, some are timid, some are friendly, some want to be left alone.

**Rabbits** are quiet, gentle animals that can be startled easily. A rabbit picked up roughly or dropped can break its own spine from kicking in fear. Rabbits need to be handled calmly and supported properly — one hand under the chest, one hand supporting the hindquarters. A rabbit that is handled gently from a young age becomes trusting and calm.

Whatever animals you work with, the first step is watching. Before you pick them up, before you try to feed them from your hand, spend time just watching. How do they move? What do they do when they are relaxed? What do they do when something startles them? What does a healthy, active animal look like?

### Part 2: The Daily Routine

Animal care follows a routine. The same things happen every day, in roughly the same order, at roughly the same time. Animals do best with consistency — they learn your routine and respond to it.

**Morning (every day, first thing):**
1. Check water. Is it clean? Is it full? Dump dirty water and refill with clean water. Animals need clean water every single day. This is non-negotiable.
2. Check food. Fill the feeder with the appropriate amount of feed. Not too much (it attracts rodents and gets dirty) and not too little (hungry animals are stressed animals).
3. Look at the animals. Are they active? Are they eating and drinking? Does anything look wrong — a bird sitting fluffed up and still, a rabbit not moving to eat? If something looks wrong, tell your mentor immediately.
4. Collect eggs (if you have laying hens). Eggs should be collected at least once a day to keep them clean and prevent hens from eating them.

**Evening (every day):**
1. Check water again. Refill if needed.
2. Make sure all animals are in their secure housing before dark. Predators — raccoons, foxes, hawks, weasels, neighborhood dogs — are the greatest threat to small animals. A coop door left open at night can mean dead chickens by morning.
3. Check that housing is secure. No holes in fencing, no gaps a predator could squeeze through.

**This happens every day.** Not most days. Not when you remember. Every day. Weekends. Holidays. Rainy days. Days when you are sick. Days when you would rather do something else. The animals need you every day, and you made a promise when you took responsibility for them.

### Part 3: Handling Animals Correctly

**Picking up a chicken:** Approach calmly. Reach under the bird with both hands, one on each side, pinning the wings gently against the body. Lift smoothly. Hold the bird against your body with one arm supporting from underneath. A chicken that is held firmly but gently will calm down. A chicken that is grabbed by one wing or one leg is in pain and will panic.

**Picking up a rabbit:** Approach the rabbit calmly. Place one hand under its chest, behind the front legs. Place the other hand under the hindquarters, supporting the back end. Lift smoothly and hold against your body. Never pick up a rabbit by its ears. Never let a rabbit's back legs dangle unsupported — a kicking rabbit can injure its spine.

**General rules for all animals:**
- Move slowly and speak softly. Sudden movements and loud noises scare animals.
- Support the animal's weight properly. An animal that feels insecure will panic.
- Put the animal down gently. Do not drop it.
- If an animal is struggling or scared, put it down and try again later. Forcing the interaction makes the animal more frightened, not less.
- Wash your hands after handling animals. Always.

### Part 4: Where Food Comes From

The animals you are caring for produce food. Chickens lay eggs. If you have meat chickens or rabbits, they will eventually be slaughtered for food. This is not something to hide from or pretend is not happening.

**Eggs** come from hens. A hen starts laying at about 5-6 months old. She lays an egg roughly every 25-27 hours during her productive period. The eggs you collect from your chickens are fresher, more nutritious, and produced by animals you know are well cared for — which is more than most people can say about the eggs they buy at a store.

**Meat** comes from animals that were alive. This is a fact, not a problem. Humans have raised animals for food for at least 10,000 years. The important thing is not whether animals are used for food — they are, and always have been — but whether they are treated well while they are alive and killed quickly and without unnecessary suffering when the time comes.

You may see an animal slaughtered. This is not done to upset you. It is done because understanding the full cycle — from living animal to food on the table — is part of knowing where your food comes from. A person who eats meat but has never faced the reality of how meat is produced has an incomplete understanding of their own food system.

### Part 5: Keeping Animals Safe and Healthy

**The five things every animal needs:**
1. **Clean water** — every day, always available, always fresh
2. **Appropriate food** — the right feed for the species, in the right amount
3. **Shelter** — protection from rain, wind, extreme heat, and extreme cold
4. **Space** — enough room to move, exercise, and express normal behavior (chickens need to scratch and dust-bathe; rabbits need to hop and stretch)
5. **Protection from predators** — secure housing, especially at night

**Signs of a healthy animal:**
- Active, alert, moving around normally
- Eating and drinking regularly
- Clear eyes, clean nostrils
- Smooth feathers (chickens) or clean fur (rabbits)
- Normal droppings

**Signs something may be wrong:**
- Sitting still, fluffed up, not moving (chickens)
- Not eating or drinking
- Discharge from eyes or nostrils
- Diarrhea
- Limping or not bearing weight on a leg
- Separating from the group (a sick animal often moves away from the flock)

If you see any of these signs, tell your mentor right away. Early detection of health problems is the difference between a treatable issue and a dead animal.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Daily Care Reliability
**Developing well:** The child performs morning animal care without being reminded, or with minimal prompting. They check water, fill feeders, collect eggs, and observe the animals as a natural part of their morning routine. They take visible pride in the animals' condition.

**Developing slowly:** The child must be reminded every day. Animal care feels like a chore imposed from outside rather than a responsibility they own. They rush through the tasks without observing the animals.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the routine integrated into the child's daily structure? Animal care should happen at the same time every day, connected to another routine event (after breakfast, before school). If it is floating randomly in the schedule, it will be forgotten. Also: does the child see the results of their care? A child who collects eggs and then eats them for breakfast understands the connection between care and reward. A child whose eggs disappear into the kitchen without acknowledgment misses the feedback loop.

### 2. Animal Handling
**Developing well:** The child approaches animals calmly, picks them up with correct technique, holds them securely, and puts them down gently. The animals are relaxed during handling. The child reads the animal's body language and adjusts accordingly.

**Developing slowly:** The child is fearful, rough, or inconsistent in handling. Animals are stressed during interactions. The child grabs rather than supports, or avoids handling entirely.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child had enough practice? Confident handling requires repetition. Ten handling sessions is a minimum. If fear is the issue, start with observation and progress to brief, gentle contact. If roughness is the issue, demonstrate correct technique again and supervise every handling interaction until the child's instincts are correct. Do not allow rough handling to continue — the animal suffers and the child learns bad habits.

### 3. Health Observation
**Developing well:** The child notices when an animal looks or behaves differently — "That chicken is just sitting there, she usually runs around" or "The rabbit's eye looks funny today." They report observations without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The child does not look at the animals during care — they fill water and feed mechanically without observing. They cannot describe what normal behavior looks like.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling observation? When you check on the animals, narrate what you see: "Look how bright her eyes are today — that's a healthy hen." "See how he's eating eagerly? Good appetite means good health." Your narration teaches the child what to look for.

### 4. Understanding of the Food Cycle
**Developing well:** The child can explain that eggs come from their chickens and that meat comes from animals that were raised and slaughtered. They discuss this factually, without distress or avoidance.

**Developing slowly:** The child avoids the topic, becomes distressed, or has not been exposed to the conversation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the conversation happened yet? If not, and the child is ready, have it. If the child is genuinely not ready (very young, highly sensitive), table it — but do not skip it permanently. Every child in this framework eventually engages with the full food cycle honestly.

### 5. Welfare Understanding
**Developing well:** The child can name what their animals need (water, food, shelter, space, safety) and can identify when something is wrong with the setup — "The water is dirty" or "There's a hole in the fence."

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot articulate what the animals need beyond food and water. They do not notice environmental problems.

**Diagnostic questions:** Walk through the animal housing together and ask the child to evaluate it: "Is everything the animals need here? What could be better?" This guided assessment builds the evaluative habit.
